docker-6._vuln_deploy_note: Real files provided (Dockerfile + docker-compose.yml). Three containers now: 'apache' (build: ., the vulnerable mod_proxy/AJP front door, port 8080->80), 'tomcat' (vulhub/tomcat:8.5.19, its originally-intended proxy backend, no exposed port), and 'flag-vault' (new -- a minimal internal-only static file server, no exposed port at all, not referenced anywhere in apache's vhost config).
docker-6._flag_note: The flag was originally on docker-10, delivered by an unrelated standalone service with no connection to this vuln. Moved here per design decision: the flag (same value, FLAG{eab5e71d80bdc38bdddd}, same deploy.env/API-key content as before) now lives on 'flag-vault', which has no host port mapping and isn't in apache's ProxyPass config -- it's only reachable from inside docker-6's own compose network. Retrieving it requires using the CVE-2021-40438 SSRF to make apache proxy to flag-vault:8000/deploy.env instead of its configured tomcat:8009 target. I built the vulnerable environment and the internal target; I did not write or include the actual SSRF payload/request needed to trigger the redirect -- that's the intended challenge for whoever plays this.

docker-10._note: No longer a flag node as of this redesign -- the flag moved to docker-6's internal flag-vault service, gated behind CVE-2021-40438 instead of being an unrelated standalone service. docker-10 is back to being a plain, unremarkable filler host, matching what the raw topology data (vulnerabilities_by_node) always said about it anyway -- it only carried a flag before because of the XML's separate FlagSequencing metadata, which this redesign intentionally overrides. The now-unused git-deploy-key-repo service files are left in place under docker/vuln_node/files/ rather than deleted, in case you want to reuse that pattern elsewhere.

topology._topology_note: No routers, no r2r links, no routing plan, no traffic flows in this scenario's XML (routers: [], routing_plan: {}, r2r_subnets: [], Traffic section explicit_count=0/weight_sum=0). All 10 docker hosts sit on one flat switch (sw-11, 172.23.255.0/24) with no router in between -- topology-routers.yml/topology-links.yml/topology-routing.yml/topology-traffic.yml are all omitted from playbooks.yml for this scenario rather than included as no-ops.

hitl._note: XML has HardwareInLoop enabled=true, attachment='existing_router' on ens19 (10.0.0.21/24) -- but this topology has zero routers, so there's nothing for it to attach to. Per your decision, HITL is skipped entirely for this scenario (treated as disabled) rather than adding a router just to host it. topology-hitl.yml omitted from playbooks.yml.
